'''James Richard "Richie" Blackmore''' (born 2 July 1969) is a [[rugby league]] coach,<ref name="Coach Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> and former professional footballer who represented [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]]. His position of preference was as a {{rlp|ce}}.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

==Early years==
An [[Otahuhu Leopards|Otahuhu]] junior, Blackmore also played [[rugby union]] until he was 21.<ref name=union /> He was regarded as a promising loose forward.<ref name=coffey>Coffey and Wood ''The Kiwis: 100 Years of International Rugby League'' {{ISBN|1-86971-090-8}}</ref>

==Castleford==
Blackmore joined [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] at the start of the 1991-92 season and quickly established himself in the team. He played 111 senior games for the club, leaving at the end of the 1994{{endash}}1995 season. It was in Castleford that Blackmore had an off field incident with [[Tawera Nikau]] that resulted in Nikau refusing to play for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team]] if Blackmore was in the same team.<ref>Richard Becht [http://www.fishpond.co.nz/Books/Sports_Recreation/Rugby/product_info/239657/ Standing Tall: The Tawera Nikau Story] 8 October 2004</ref>

Richie Blackmore played at {{rlp|ce}} and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]]'s 12{{endash}}28 defeat by [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1992 Challenge Cup]] Final during the [[1991–92 Rugby Football League season|1991–92 season]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on Saturday 2 May 1992, in front of a crowd of 77,386.<ref name="2nd May 1992: Wigan 28 Castleford 12 (Challenge Cup Final)">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=3066|title=2nd May 1992: Wigan 28 Castleford 12 (Challenge Cup Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2016|access-date=1 January 2017|archive-date=15 February 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170215030604/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=3066|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Richie Blackmore played at {{rlp|ce}} in [[Castleford Tigers]]' 33{{endash}}2 victory over [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1993–94 League Cup (rugby league)|1993–94 Regal Trophy]] Final during the [[1993–94 Rugby Football League season|1993–94 season]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], [[Leeds]] on Saturday 22 January 1994.<ref name="Wigan 2 - 33 Castleford">{{cite web|url=http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/viewreport.php?page=report&id=775|title=Wigan 2 - 33 Castleford|publisher=thecastlefordtigers.co.uk|date=31 December 2011|access-date=1 January 2012}}</ref>

==Auckland Warriors==
Blackmore joined the new [[Auckland Warriors]] side in 1995, however he missed the opening rounds due to the overlap with the British season. As a result, he found it hard to secure a place in the starting line up and played only ten first grade games in 1995.

Blackmore played another seventeen games for the Auckland Warriors in 1996 before deciding to return to England.

==Leeds Rhinos==
Blackmore joined the [[Leeds Rhinos]] in 1997, joining the [[Super League]] competition. He played for Leeds Rhinos at {{rlp|ce}}, scoring their sole try in the [[1998 Super League Grand Final]] loss to [[Wigan Warriors]]. He went on to play 45 first grade games for the club.

In mid-1999 Blackmore suffered a serious leg injury that kept him out for most of the season.<ref>[https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-blackmore-doubt-forces-leeds-to-spend-1091861.html Blackmore doubt forces Leeds to spend] ''The Independent'', 6 May 1999</ref><ref>[http://www.bookrags.com/highbeam/rugby-league-blackmore-pulls-leeds-back-20000311-hb/ Blackmore pulls Leeds back from brink of upset Leeds 42 Dewsbury 10] ''The Independent'', 11 March 2000</ref>

In 2000, while recovering from injury, Blackmore played rugby union for the [[Leeds Tykes]] against [[Sale Sharks|Sale]] in Round Four of the [[Tetley's Bitter Cup]].<ref name=union>[https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby/rugby-union/leeds-find-that-two-into-one-does-go-636127.html Leeds find that two into one does go]{{dead link|date=August 2021|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} ''The Independent'', 2 January 2000</ref>

==Return & Retirement==
At the end of the 2000 season Blackmore signed a one-year deal with the renamed [[New Zealand Warriors]] who were looking for an experience back to help shape their youthful squad. Blackmore played eight [[National Rugby League|first grade]] games for the club, before retiring at the end of the season.<ref>[http://www.highbeam.com/doc/1P2-17324407.html Blackmore boomerangs back to Warriors]{{dead link|date=February 2019|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} ''Sunday Star Times'', 19 November 2000</ref>

He continued to play in the [[Auckland Rugby League]] competition, moving into the second row. In 2005 he was the [[player-coach]] of the [[Manurewa Marlins]] when they won the Fox Memorial. He retired properly at the end of that season, taking up the head coach position at [[Otahuhu Leopards|Otahuhu]] in 2006.<ref>Coffey, John and Bernie Wood ''Auckland, 100 years of rugby league, 1909–2009'', 2009. {{ISBN|978-1-86969-366-4}}, p. 347.</ref>

==International career==
Richie Blackmore was a [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] international, making his début for the Kiwis in 1991. Blackmore represented the Kiwis in both the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup|1995]] and [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]]s.

==Coaching==
After retiring from playing Blackmore remained involved with the Warriors, and was often seen running out water to the team during matches.

In 2006 Blackmore accepted a role as head coach of the [[Otahuhu Leopards]] in the [[Auckland Rugby League]] competition.<ref>[http://www.times.co.nz/cms/sport/sports_news/2006/06/art100011815.php Coaches keen to extend Otahuhu's proud record] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110525072612/http://www.times.co.nz/cms/sport/sports_news/2006/06/art100011815.php |date=25 May 2011 }} ''Tamaki and Districts Times'', 7 June 2006</ref> He held this position until the end of 2010, leading the team to a Fox Memorial grand final victory over the [[Mt Albert Lions]].<ref>"What they said", ''Super League'', Issue 1 2010. p. 17</ref>

In 2008 Blackmore joined the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori team]] as assistant coach.<ref>{{usurped|1=[https://archive.today/20090723175315/http://www.rlcup.com/news.php?newsid=16400 NZ Maori name coaching staff for World Cup curtain raiser]}} ''rlcup.com'', 14 June 2008</ref> He was co-coach in 2010 along with [[Mark Horo]].

In 2011 Blackmore was appointed as head coach of the [[Auckland Vulcans]] in the [[NSW Cup]], and coached the team to the Grand Final.<ref>[http://www.sportingpulse.com/club_info.cgi?client=16-4975-91707-90473-0&sID=74125&&news_task=DETAIL&articleID=14141902&sectionID=74125 Vulcans Coach for 2011] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121012020529/http://www.sportingpulse.com/club_info.cgi?client=16-4975-91707-90473-0&sID=74125&&news_task=DETAIL&articleID=14141902&sectionID=74125 |date=12 October 2012 }} ''aucklandvulcans.co.nz'', 9 December 2010</ref>

In 2012 he was to be appointed coach of the [[Leigh Centurions]], however visa concerns meant he was instead appointed as the club's Business Development Manager, starting his position in April 2012.<ref>[https://archive.today/20120720102552/http://uk.eurosport.yahoo.com/02022012/63/leigh-appoint-rowley.html Leigh appoint Rowley] ''Yahoo! Sport'', 2 February 2012</ref>

In 2014 he coached the [[Papakura Sea Eagles]] in the [[Auckland Rugby League]] competition.<ref name=teamlistjuly5>[http://www.sportsground.co.nz/files/site/305/4/Pdf/140704131704EMPSNTDF.pdf Team Lists Round 13 - July 5] ''aucklandleague.co.nz''</ref> He coached [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] in 2015.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aucklandleague.co.nz/news-1|title=Auckland Representative Side Named to Face NZ Maori Residents|access-date=21 May 2015|archive-date=20 May 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150520131905/http://www.aucklandleague.co.nz/news-1|url-status=dead}}</ref>
